free_text: Level Scheme of 153Sm Based on (n,gamma), (n,e-), and
beta-Decay Experiments
In-pile facility at the Argonne CP-5
reseach reactor.
Reactor core with a thermal neutron flux ~3E+13
n/(cm2.s).
Reactor spectrum.
Samples of enriched 149Sm and 150Sm used to identify
and remove gamma lines from 149Sm and 150Sm (n,gamma)
reactions close to lines from the 152Sm(n,gamma)153Sm
reaction.
IS
7.7-m Argonne bent-crystal
spectrometer.
180 mg of Sm203 (enriched 99.06% in 152Sm) enclosed
in an Mg(85%)-Al(15%) holder which constrained the
sample to a volume 7-cm high, 1-cm deep, 0.017-cm
wide.
Bank of NaI crystals used to detect the low
energy gamma-ray spectrum as a function of Bragg
angle of the bent-crystal spectrometer. 2-mm- and
4-mm-thick bent quartz crystals employed in the
spectrometer.
On basis of previously published studies 103-keV line
was assumed to have an intensity of 28 gamma rays per
100 neutron captures when the beta-decay rate was in
equilibrium with the neutron-capture rate.
Gamma-ray energies based on the previously established
energy calibration of the bent-crystal spectrometer;
additionally checked in comparing the energies
obtained for some of the strong lines in the
149Sm(n,gamma)150Sm spectrum with published values.
Energies and intensities represent an average taken
from the analysis of a series of 4-10 runs over the
spectrum.
Each line approximated in the computer analysis of the
data with a Gaussian curve when well-resolved regions
of the spectrum were fitted. A more elaborate line
shape was used for each diffraction peak in the case
of fitting complex doublet and triplet structures,
Data corrected for the presence of gammas from the
149Sm, 150Sm, 151Sm, and 154Sm (n,gamma) reactions.
Also identified and removed were the transitions
produced by the 153Sm(n,g)154Sm, and 153Eu(n,g)154Eu
reactions by observing the variations in their
intensities as a function of time.
Uncertainties in gamma-ray energies; do not
include a possible systematic error estimated as
large as 1 part in 10,000.
Relative uncertainties of line intensities;
do not include any error in normalization procedure.
Data taken from Table II of reference.
85 mg of Sm203 (enriched 99.06% in 152Sm) enclosed
in a carbon source holder.
NaI crystals placed on opposite sides of the Ge(Li)
detector were used in coincidence in some runs to
identify double-escape peaks.
Data consisted of 14 separate runs.
Data runs identifying double-escape peaks in spectrum
made with a gate that recorded the spectrum in the
Ge(Li) detector only in coincidence with NaI crystals.
Absolute values of the gamma energies obtained by
measuring the relative energies of the stronger lines
in carbon and nitrogen and adjusting them for the best
fit with the published values (G.E.Thomas et al.,
Nucl.Instr.Meth. 56, 325, 1967).
Data corrected for gamma transitions associated with
background sources, for gamma lines of 150Sm and 151Sm
and for fast neutrons scattered by the sample.
Uncertainties in gamma-ray energies;
reflect only the errors associated with the location
of the centroid of the peak and the calibration of the
energy per channel.
Relative uncertainties of line intensities;
reflect errors on the relative intensities only and do
not include a possible systematic error of 25% in thei
absolute values.
Data taken from Table IV of reference.
